
# Introduction

## Purpose

This report summarises the data and results of the analyses for main outcomes of the OPTIMUM
trial. 
The report includes results for:

- participant disposition
- participant baseline characteristics
- skin prick tests
- food allergy
- eczema
- safety
- reactogenicity
- immunogenicity

For full details of the trial processes, objectives, and outcomes refer to the protocol
and statistical analysis plan.

## Interventions

Participants were randomised 1:1 to receive either:

- Combined Diphtheria (D)-Tetanus (T)-acellular Pertussis (aP), Hepatitis B (HepB), Inactivated Poliovirus (IPV) and Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) vaccine (DTaPHepB-IPV-Hib) administered at approximately 2 months, together with the standard schedule of vaccines (referred to as 3-aP + aP or aP for short), OR
- Combined Diphtheria (D)-Tetanus (T)-whole cell Pertussis (wP), Hepatitis B (HepB) and Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) vaccine (DTwP-HepB- Hib) in place of DTaP-HepB-IPV-Hib at approximately 2 months, together with the standard schedule of vaccines (referred to as 1-wP/2-aP + aP or wP for short).

## Trial design

Originally, a sample size of 3,000 participants was planned. Under the adaptive design, as outlined in the protocol and statistical analysis plan, we estimated the trial had 85% power to detect a reduction in food allergy from 10% to 7% with type I error of 5%. If $\delta$ denotes the difference in probability of food allergy under each intervention, then trial success required that $\delta$ was very likely to be less than zero, specifically that $\Pr(\delta < 0) > 0.95$.

The maximum sample size was subsequently revised down to 2,000 participants due to feasibility constraints. The trial was stopped early due to logistical issues with 972 participants randomised.

## Trial outcomes

A summary of trial outcomes is provided below.

### Primary outcome (1) {#sec-primary-outcome}

IgE mediated food allergy with evidence of food sensitisation on skin prick test (SPT) by 12 months old and confirmed (where necessary) by medically supervised oral food challenge(s) (OFC). For the purpose of this trial, if either of the following conditions are met, the study participant will be considered to have reached the primary endpoint:

1.  Unequivocal evidence of IgE mediated food allergy, defined as:
    i.  A positive OFC with evidence of sensitisation on SPT to the food of interest *OR*
    ii. History of clinician-confirmed food anaphylaxis, with symptoms affecting at least two of the following: skin, gastrointestinal tract, respiratory tract, cardiovascular system; *AND* evidence of sensitisation on SPT to the food of interest.
2.  Highly probable IgE-mediated food allergy defined as a history of food allergic reaction (consistent with PRACTALL criteria), with evidence of sensitisation on SPT to the food of interest.

We will report on the number and proportion who satisfy this composite definition, and the number and proportion satisfying each of the three sub-components as per a hierarchy in order of 1.i, 1.ii, 2.

### Secondary outcomes (2) {#sec-secondary-outcomes}

a.  A history of parent-reported clinician-diagnosed new onset eczema by **6 months** *AND* a positive SPT to any allergen by approximately 12 months old.
b.  A history of parent-reported clinician-diagnosed new onset eczema by **12 months** *AND* a positive SPT to any allergen by approximately 12 months old.
c.  An SPT **\>1mm** greater than the negative control to at least one allergen by approximately 12 months of age
d.  An SPT **≥3mm** greater than the negative control to at least one allergen by approximately 12 months of age

### Reactogenicity

Solicited and unsolicited adverse events following immunisation were collected on diary cards in the 7 days after administration of pertussis-containing vaccines for the first 300 participants at PCH. Solicited local adverse events included injection site pain, erythema, induration and swelling; the solicited systemic adverse events recorded were fever ≥38°C (axillary), drowsiness, irritability, anorexia, vomiting, diarrhoea and restlessness.

The definition and intensity grading for fever, diarrhoea and local reactions (except for immunisation site pain) followed the Brighton Collaboration guidelines. For other solicited adverse events, intensity grading scales based on impact on daily activities were used (grade 0 or absent; grade 1 if easily tolerated; grade 2, sufficiently discomforting to interfere with normal everyday activities and grade 3, if prevents normal everyday activities or requires significant medical intervention).

### Immunological outcomes

Total, tetanus toxoid and food allergen-specific IgE were measured in plasma at the age of 6, 7 and 19 months (stage 1 only), using ImmunoCAP total and specific IgE assays (Thermo Fisher Scientific/Phadia, Uppsala, Sweden).

Vaccine antigen-specific IgG titres were measured immediately before and 21–35 days after the 6-month (stage 1 only) and 18-month vaccine doses. These antibody levels were measured using a multiplex fluorescent bead assay. For stage 1, sera were tested for IgG against B. pertussis antigens (pertussis toxin (PT), filamentous haemagglutinin (FHA), pertactin (PRN), fimbriae agglutinogens 2–3 (FIM2/3)), tetanus toxoid (TT), capsular polysaccharide of Hib (Hib-PRP) and each of the 13-valent pneumococcal vaccine serotypes (PnPs). For stage 2, sera were only tested for pertussis toxin-specific IgG in the first additional 150 study participants enrolled at PCH.

### Additional outcome details

The protocol defined allergen panel for skin prick tests included cow’s milk, hen’s egg, peanut, cashew, house dust mite (Dermatophagoides pteronyssinus), cat, rye grass (Lolium perenne), a positive control (10 mg/mL histamine) and negative control (saline). In addition to these, further allergens could also be tested at parental request or at discretion of the site PI. When the outcomes say "at least one allergen" [i.e. for eczema for positive SPT] it was meant any allergen, including any additional allergens tested beyond the standard panel.

For SPT's, the specification of "by approximately 12-months old" was taken to mean "up to 18-months old". Under the original protocol, SPTs were scheduled to occur at 18-months of age, but following an early revision, scheduled SPTs were revised to occur at 12-months of age. Unscheduled SPTs could still occur up to 18 months of age.

For both food allergy and eczema, sensitisation on (positive) SPT was defined to be a result more than 1mm greater than negative control by 18 months of age. For food allergy, the positive SPT needed to match the specific food allergen. 

For eczema, the age requirement (by 6 or 12 months of age) was determined by the "age at which allergy first occurred" (`outageval`). To be considered "new-onset" eczema, this age at which allergy first occurred needed to be after the infants enrolment date. Infants with clinician-diagnosed eczema first occurring prior to enrolment were to be excluded as cases which were not "new-onset eczema".

## Analyses

Unless otherwise stated, all summaries and analyses group participants according to their assigned treatment group and use all available data irrespective of protocol deviations or other intercurrent events (ITT).

### Primary outcome {#sec-primary-analysis}

The primary analysis was to assume independent Binomial models for occurrence of food allergy under each treatment group with uniform priors on the outcome probabilities. 

::: {.callout-note}

## Primary model

Let $\theta_{\text{wP}}$ and $\theta_{\text{aP}}$ denote the probability of the outcome amongst infants who are assigned to the respective strategy and $\delta = \theta_{\text{wP}} - \theta_{\text{aP}}$ the difference in probablity. If $n_{\text{wP}}$ and $n_{\text{aP}}$ infants were assigned to each respective strategy, with $y_{\text{wP}}$ and $y_{\text{aP}}$ events observed, than from our assumed model we estimate that $\theta_j \sim \text{Beta}(1 + y_j, 1 + n_j - y_j)$ quantifies our uncertainty and $\delta$ the difference between these.

:::

A secondary analysis was to assume a logistic regression model for occurrence of food allergy adjusting for baseline factors: site, sex, breastfeeding status, birth order, parental income, mode of delivery, and family history of atopic disease.

### Secondary clinical outcomes

Analyses of secondary outcomes are analogous to those for the primary outcome, both the primary and secondary models.

\clearpage
